did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a two-stage deep neural network approach for melanoma classification in dermoscopy images. Specifically, it employs a U-Net for lesion segmentation and an ensemble of five deep convolutional neural networks with Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks for classification, evaluated on the ISIC 2017 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'ORIGINAL RESEARCH' in the header. It presents a novel two-stage deep neural network method for melanoma classification, detailing specific methodologies (U-Net segmentation, ensemble of five CNNs with SE blocks), implementation details (Keras, ISIC 2017 dataset), and experimental results (accuracy, AUC metrics), which are characteristic of an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: Two-Stage Deep Neural Network via Ensemble Learning for Melanoma Classification
brief_summary: This study proposes a two-stage deep neural network framework for melanoma classification using dermoscopy images to address the challenges of early diagnosis. The method first employs a U-Net architecture to segment lesion areas and generate masks, which are then used to resize and focus the input images. In the second stage, an ensemble of five deep convolutional neural networks (Inception-v3, DenseNet169, ResNet50, Inception-ResNet-v2, and Xception) with Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks is utilized to extract features and classify the images. The classification results from these models are integrated using a novel ensemble network with locally connected layers to produce the final prediction. The approach was evaluated on the ISIC 2017 challenge dataset, which contains 2,750 dermoscopy images across three classes: melanoma, benign nevi, and seborrheic keratosis. The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 0.909 and an AUC of 0.911, demonstrating superior performance compared to individual networks and other ensemble methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for classification; part of the ISIC 2017 dataset (374 training, 30 validation, 117 testing images). Also used in binary classification subtask 'melanoma or others'.
One of the three classes in the ISIC 2017 dataset (1,372 training, 78 validation, 393 testing images). Used as a comparator class in multi-class classification.

One of the three classes in the ISIC 2017 dataset (254 training, 42 validation, 90 testing images). Used as a comparator class in multi-class classification and in the binary subtask 'seborrheic keratosis or others'.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma, benign nevi, seborrheic keratosis) and binary labels (melanoma vs. others, seborrheic keratosis vs. others).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification (3 classes: MM, SK, BN) and binary classification tasks (melanoma or others, seborrheic keratosis or others) using dermoscopy images. The model outputs class predictions via a softmax classifier. While U-Net is used for segmentation to generate masks for preprocessing, the primary AI task evaluated and reported is classification, with metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 challenge dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset provided by The International Society for Digital Imaging of the Skin (ISIC) for the 2017 challenge, containing dermoscopy images with segmentation ground truth (binary masks).

use.training: Used for training the U-Net segmentation model and the five deep convolutional neural networks (Inception-v3, DenseNet169, ResNet50, Inception-ResNet-v2, Xception) with Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (early stopping with patience of 10 epochs).
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluating the final classification performance (accuracy, AUC, precision, recall, F1-score) of the proposed ensemble method.
use.other: The dataset also provided binary masks for segmentation ground truth used to train the U-Net model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

multi_class_classification.description: Classification of three classes: Melanoma (MM), Seborrheic Keratosis (SK), and Benign Nevi (BN).
multi_class_classification.notes: The proposed ensemble method achieved the highest AUC (0.913) among single networks and ensemble methods for multi-class classification. Segmentation improved performance significantly compared to no segmentation (ACC 0.791 vs 0.698).

binary_classification.description: Average results of two binary classification tasks: 'melanoma or others' and 'seborrheic keratosis or others'.
binary_classification.notes: The proposed ensemble method achieved the highest Precision (0.859), F1-score (0.828), and AUC (0.911) among all compared methods. It outperformed the best single network (Xception) in AUC by 1%.

comparison_with_top_challenge_methods.description: Comparison of the proposed method against the top five ISIC 2017 challenge methods and other recent state-of-the-art methods.
comparison_with_top_challenge_methods.notes: The proposed method achieved the highest ACC (0.909) and Precision (0.859) among all listed methods. Its AUC (0.911) was lower than Datta et al. (0.959) and Zhang et al. (0.958), but higher than Top 1-5 and Xie et al. Its F1-score (0.828) was lower than the best score (implied to be higher than 0.828, specifically noted as 0.023 lower than the best score in text, though Top 1 has 0.851). The text notes that most challenge methods used external images, which this study did not.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-stage deep neural network framework consisting of a U-Net for segmentation and an ensemble of five deep convolutional neural networks (Inception-v3, DenseNet169, ResNet50, Inception-ResNet-v2, and Xception) with Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks for classification, integrated via a custom ensemble network with locally connected layers.
architecture_used.pretraining: The five classification networks (Inception-v3, DenseNet169, ResNet50, Inception-ResNet-v2, Xception) were pre-trained on ImageNet.

stage_1_segmentation.purpose: Segment lesion area to generate a mask for resizing and focusing input images.

stage_2_classification.modifications: Added Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks to emphasize informative features.
stage_2_classification.feature_extraction: Full connected layer of 128-dimension used to generate final feature vector.

ensemble_network.structure: Neural network classifier with two locally connected layers and a softmax layer.
ensemble_network.input: Classification probabilities from the five base networks.
ensemble_network.rationale: Locally connected layers used instead of fully connected layers to extract features for each class separately, preventing influence from other classes.

classification_or_output_head.ensemble_head: Two locally connected layers followed by a softmax layer

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Ensemble Network (with locally connected layers)
preprocessing_techniques: Lesion segmentation using U-Net to generate binary masks
Cropping original images using segmentation masks to focus on lesion areas
Resizing cropped images to a fixed size of 224 x 224 pixels
Data augmentation via rotation (180°), horizontal and vertical flipping, and random translation (10% in height and width directions)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random translation moving image height and width direction by 10%

conclusions: The proposed two-stage deep neural network framework, combining U-Net segmentation with an ensemble of five deep convolutional neural networks (Inception-v3, DenseNet169, ResNet50, Inception-ResNet-v2, and Xception) enhanced by Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks and integrated via a custom ensemble network with locally connected layers, effectively classifies melanoma in dermoscopy images.

The method achieved an accuracy of 0.909 and an AUC of 0.911 on the ISIC 2017 challenge dataset, demonstrating superior performance compared to individual base networks and traditional averaging ensemble methods.

Lesion segmentation using U-Net significantly improved classification performance, increasing accuracy from 0.698 to 0.791 and AUC from 0.781 to 0.883 when using the Inception-v3 baseline, confirming that focusing on lesion areas mitigates issues related to varying lesion sizes and blurred boundaries.

The custom ensemble network with locally connected layers outperformed the average ensemble method, achieving a 2% improvement in AUC over the best single network (Xception) in multi-class classification tasks.

The proposed ensemble method achieved the highest accuracy (0.909) and precision (0.859) among compared methods in binary classification tasks (melanoma vs. others, seborrheic keratosis vs. others), although its AUC was lower than some state-of-the-art methods that utilized external images.
The use of Squeeze-and-Excitation blocks helped emphasize informative features, contributing to the model's ability to handle inter-class similarity and intra-class variation in skin lesions.

The study acknowledges that while the proposed method performs well, most top-performing methods in the ISIC 2017 challenge utilized external images, which were not used in this study, suggesting a potential area for future improvement.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2017 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (1,372 benign nevi vs. 374 melanoma in training), potentially biasing the model towards the majority class.

The authors explicitly acknowledge that top-performing methods in the ISIC 2017 challenge utilized external images for training, whereas this study did not, limiting direct comparability of performance metrics like AUC against those external-augmented models.

The model's recall for melanoma (0.808) is notably lower than its precision (0.859) and lower than the best challenge method (Top 1 recall 0.856), indicating a potential clinical risk of false negatives in melanoma detection.

The ensemble network architecture is extremely lightweight (only 423 parameters) compared to the base networks (e.g., Inception-ResNet-v2 with 54.87M parameters), suggesting the complexity lies in the base feature extractors rather than the fusion mechanism.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported to validate the significance of performance improvements over baseline or competing methods.

The dataset distribution is uneven across training, validation, and test sets, particularly for the minority classes (Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis), which may affect the generalizability of the results.
The study does not report on external validation on independent datasets, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical deployment robustness.
